_comment: SC-001a / SC-002 baseline — manually captured, never auto-updated. Re-capture requires a reviewed commit.

reference_machine.cpu: x86_64 under WSL2 (AMD/Intel laptop-class, >=3GHz base). See git log for exact hardware at capture time.

lint_10kb._note: Values are criterion 95% confidence interval bounds (advisory; reference-machine measurement). Reproduces ~620µs on WSL2 dev machines, so the +10% percentage gate against `upper_ci_us` would always fail there; SC-001 is well within reach in either case (16ms target). Per Phase 4 review L6: this bench uses the absolute `drift_alert_upper_ci_us` field below instead of the per-machine percentage gate. Anything over 1ms on the strict path is suspicious and worth investigating; the 16ms absolute target remains the SC-001 contract.

decoder_10kb_one_mangled_region._note: Engine::lint with `with_deep_scan()` on a 10KB document containing exactly one mangled portion (`(SERCET//NF)`) plus normal valid markings + prose. `SERCET` is the deliberate misspelling the SC-002 bench feeds the engine to force the decoder to fire — it is NOT a typo to be corrected. Captured on a WSL2 dev machine; reference-machine re-capture is welcome but not required — the SC-002 18ms absolute target is the load-bearing gate, the per-bench `upper_ci_us` exists for the +10% regression-detection step in `scripts/bench-check.sh`.

lint_scaling._note: SC-005 linear-scaling gate. R² is the coefficient of determination from an ordinary-least-squares fit of (input_size_bytes, mean_time_µs) across the input-size sweep in `crates/engine/benches/linear_scaling.rs` (currently 1KB → 100KB across seven points). 0.9 is the floor — substantially-better R² is normal for the strict path because per-document work is dominated by linear scanner + parser passes; a value below 0.9 usually indicates either super-linear behavior (a new quadratic somewhere) or pathological noise from the runner. Enforced by `scripts/bench-check.sh::check_linear_scaling`. Choice of input-size scaling vs. worker-count scaling is documented in the script header.
